coredata.dc:title: Enhancing photocatalytic destruction of lignin via cellulose derived carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 heterojunctions
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wei
Jiang, Chuang
Feng, Jinlong
Zhang, Liguo
Hou, Qingxi
Ji, Xingxiang
coredata.dc:description: Lignocellulosic biomass exhibits a promising potential for production of carbon materials. Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQDs) were fabricated via (NH4)2HPO4 assisted hydrothermal treatment of cellulose pulp fibers. The as-prepared N,P-CQDs were characterized by HRTEM, FTIR, fluorescence and UV–vis, and then incorporated into g-C3N4 (CN) through sonication and liquid deposition, forming N,P-CQDs/sonication treated g-C3N4 (C-SCN) composites, which were then explored as photocatalysts. The photocatalytic ability of C-SCN towards model lignin was further analyzed. The results showed that, the fluorescence intensity and photoluminescence performance of N,P-CQDs were much higher than that of CQDs; the heterojunction was successfully constructed between the composites of N,P-CQDs and SCN; the incorporation of N,P-CQDs enhanced the visible light absorption, but reduced the band gap of the composite heterojunction; the resultant photocatalysts exhibited a good photocatalytic ability of model lignin via catalyze the fracture of β–O–4′ ether bond and CC bond, i.e., the photocatalytic degradation ratio reached up to 95.5 %; and the photocatalytic reaction generated some valuable organics such as phenyl formate, benzaldehyde, and benzoic acid. This study would promote the high value-added utilization of lignocellulosic resources especially in the transformation of lignin, conforming the concept of sustainable development.

head.ce:title: Enhancing photocatalytic destruction of lignin via cellulose derived carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 heterojunctions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lignocellulosic biomass exhibits a promising potential for production of carbon materials. Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQDs) were fabricated via (NH4)2HPO4 assisted hydrothermal treatment of cellulose pulp fibers. The as-prepared N,P-CQDs were characterized by HRTEM, FTIR, fluorescence and UV–vis, and then incorporated into g-C3N4 (CN) through sonication and liquid deposition, forming N,P-CQDs/sonication treated g-C3N4 (C-SCN) composites, which were then explored as photocatalysts. The photocatalytic ability of C-SCN towards model lignin was further analyzed. The results showed that, the fluorescence intensity and photoluminescence performance of N,P-CQDs were much higher than that of CQDs; the heterojunction was successfully constructed between the composites of N,P-CQDs and SCN; the incorporation of N,P-CQDs enhanced the visible light absorption, but reduced the band gap of the composite heterojunction; the resultant photocatalysts exhibited a good photocatalytic ability of model lignin via catalyze the fracture of β–O–4′ ether bond and CC bond, i.e., the photocatalytic degradation ratio reached up to 95.5 %; and the photocatalytic reaction generated some valuable organics such as phenyl formate, benzaldehyde, and benzoic acid. This study would promote the high value-added utilization of lignocellulosic resources especially in the transformation of lignin, conforming the concept of sustainable development.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignocellulosic biomass has already been regarded as an ideal candidate to replace non-renewable mineral carbon materials including coal, petroleum and natural gas [1,2]. As one of renewable aromatic compounds, lignin ranks the second largest natural polymers after cellulose [3,4]. The complex structure of lignin makes it difficult to be transformed especially in terms of efficiency and selectively, leading to a low utilization even a huge waste [5]. The complex structure of lignin generates a large spatial hindrance for the adsorption of traditional catalysts. It is reported that a high energy barrier between the rigid surface of traditional catalysts and benzene ring of lignin severely limits its catalytic degradation [6]. By far, some traditional methods have been applying on lignin degradation including physical, chemical and biological methods, but they are severely limited due to existing problems (e.g., low selectivity and efficiency, high environmental pollution, and complex process). Thus, it is very necessary to develop novel method and catalysts to satisfy the requirements in terms of simple operation, low environmental pollution, high efficiency, and relatively mild conditions.

Vacancies g-C3N4 has become one of the most promising visible light redox catalysts [7,8], because of the unique properties, e.g., large surface area, low cost, visible light adsorption, and large number of nitrogen atoms. It is reported that the flexible 2D material g-C3N4 may provide an ideal surface for catalytic degradation of lignin [9,10]. However, g-C3N4 can only absorb sunlight with a wavelength below 450 nm, leading to a low utilization of sunlight. In addition, the electron recombination rate of g-C3N4 is fast, thus only a small amount of electrons and holes can participate in the redox reaction during the catalytic process [11–13]. Interestingly, the combination of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with g-C3N4 can significantly improve photocatalytic degradation efficiency. As a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, CQDs are mainly composed of C, H and O elements, and distribute in the range of ≤10 nm [14]. The advantages, such as abundant raw materials, easy preparation, excellent optical properties, and easy modification, ensure CQDs a wide practical applications. Recently, CQDs have already played an important role in many fields, such as fluorescent probes, photocatalysis, and biological imaging [15–17]. Among them, the excellent up conversion photoluminescence ability of CQDs can obviously promote the photocatalytic efficiency of resultant photocatalyst, realizing an effective utilization of full-spectrum sunlight [18–20]. Photochemical property is of great importance for the application of CQDs in photoelectric conversion. The luminous properties of CQDs can be improved by introducing elements such as nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) into the interior or surface of CQDs [21,22]. The outermost layer of N atom has 5 electrons, which can form a bond with carbon (C) atom. The introduction of n-type carrier can provide electrons for CQDs, and induce upward movement of Fermi energy level, improving the activity of electrons in conduction band, consequently changing the optical properties of CQDs [23]. Dong et al. [24] prepared nitrogen doped CQDs by hydrothermal method using L-cysteine and citric acid as precursors. Their results showed that the amount of N doping directly affected the fluorescence intensity, multi-color and two-photon up conversion characteristics of CQDs. In addition, P has many similarities with C in terms of structure and chemical properties. Doping P element into CQDs can increase the defects, thereby changing the chemical and optical properties. The enhancement of the optical properties of CQDs is conducive to improving the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs. In our previous work [25], nitrogen and phosphorus-doped CQDs (N,P-CQDs) were prepared from cellulose pulp, subsequently three fractions of N,P-CQDs based on molecular weight (i.e., N,P-CQDs-3500, N,P-CQDs-1000, and N,P-CQDs-500) were obtained. It is found that, 1) N,P-CQDs-3500 exhibits the strongest fluorescence intensity among them; 2) the maximum excitation/emission wavelength of N,P-CQDs-3500 is 330/412 nm/nm; 3) the absorption peak of N,P-CQDs-3500 is extended to the visible region; 4) N,P-CQDs-3500 is relatively stable under both strongly acidic and weakly alkaline environments; 5) both Fe(III) and Fe(II) ions have obvious fluorescence quenching effect on N,P-CQDs-3500. Liu et al. [26] prepared porous g-C3N4 modified with CQDs, and found that the degradation rate of diclofenac acid (DCF) for the CQDs modified g-C3N4 was 15 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4. In this process, CQDs provide a channel for the preferential transfer of photoexcited electrons on DCF molecules to the conduction band of g-C3N4 via fixing CO bonds on g-C3N4 surface.

Herein, both nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQDs) were fabricated via (NH4)2HPO4 assisted hydrothermal treatment of cellulose pulp fibers. The as-prepared N,P-CQDs were characterized by HRTEM, FTIR, fluorescence and UV–vis, and then incorporated into g-C3N4 (CN) through sonication and liquid deposition, forming N,P-CQDs/sonication treated g-C3N4 (C-SCN) composites, which were then explored as photocatalysts. The utilization of cellulose pulp fibers ensures the resultant CQDs with unique advantages such as good water solubility and high optical properties, which will expand the application field and high value-added utilization of lignocellulosic biomass, conforming to the concept of green and sustainable development.

The bleached softwood Kraft pulp (BSKP) fibers were provided by a paper mill in Shandong Province, China. First, the pulp was soaked in deionized water for 4 h at room temperature, and tore into fragments with size of 25 × 25 mm. The pulp fibers were completely dispersed according to TAPPI standard T248 sp-00. Then, the dispersed pulp fibers were collected and stored in 4 °C.

Ammonium dibasic phosphate ((NH4)2HPO4) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China. Melamine was obtained from Tianjin Kangkemei Technology Co., Ltd., China. 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethanol (PP-ol) was obtained from Jiangsu Aikang Biomedical R&D Co., Ltd., China. Acetonitrile was obtained from Meryer (Shanghai) Chemical Technology Co., Ltd., China. 4-Methylacetophenone was obtained from Tianjin Yifang Technology Co., Ltd., China. Other chemicals are of analytical grade.

The pulp fiber (12.0 g) was thoroughly dispersed in 150 mL deionized water, stirred at 1000 rpm for 30 min, then the pulp suspension was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 5 h at a stirring speed of 200 rpm. The obtained suspension was cooled to room temperature after the hydrothermal treatment, and filtered with a 0.22 μm cylindrical filter membrane to collect the filtrate. The filtrate was put into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 3500 Da, and kept in deionized water for 2 days, the deionized water was changed every 6 h during this period. Then, the remaining liquid in the dialysis bag was collected and frozen dried, i.e., CQDs. Similarly, 12.0 g of pulp and 12.0 g of (NH4)2HPO4 were thoroughly dispersed in 150 mL deionized water, and the suspensions were conducted with the above method and conditions, the N and P co-doped CQDs were obtained and named as N,P-CQDs.

FTIR spectra of the cellulose pulp, CQDs and N,P-CQDs were recorded using a spectrophotometer (Model 650, Tianjin Gangdong Sci. &Tech. Development Co., Ltd., China) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs and N,P-CQDs were obtained on a spectrophotometer (DR-6000, HACH, USA). Fluorescence spectra of CQDs and N,P-CQDs were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7100, Hitachi Co., Ltd., Japan). The elemental analysis of the cellulose pulp was carried out using an organic element analyzer (Elementar vario EL cube, Elementar, USA) and coupled with a plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (ICAP Q, Thermo Scientific, USA). The elemental composition of CQDs and N,P-CQDs were analyzed by energy spectrometer (X-MaxN20, Oxford Instruments, Britain).

Carbon nitride (g-C3N4) powder was prepared according to the literature [27]. Briefly 5.0 g melamine was placed in an alumina ceramic crucible, kept heating at 550 °C for 4 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1, and the resultant yellow powder was the g-C3N4 (CN). The resulting material was ultrasonically treated at 300 W for 2 h, dried in an oven at 80 °C, and then named as SCN. After that, 0.2 g of SCN was thoroughly dispersed in 20 mL of N,P-CQDs solution with concentration of 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0 mg/mL, respectively, and kept ultrasonically treating at 30 °C for 1 h. The mixed solution containing N,P-CQDs and SCN was frozen dried, forming the composite (C-SCN), which were labeled as C-SCN-1, C-SCN-2, and C-SCN-3 according to concentration of N,P-CQDs (i.e., 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 mg/mL), respectively. Fig. 1 shows the schematic illustration of C-SCN composite fabrication. Then, the as-prepared C-SCN was characterized and explored as a photocatalyst in the subsequent sections.

X-ray diffraction spectrum (XRD) was measured with RIGAKU SmartLab (Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a ThermoFischer ESCALAB 250Xi (USA). FTIR, photoluminescence (PL), SEM-EDS analysis of test samples were conducted. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were obtained using a UV-3600 plus spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., China) by using BaSO4 as a reference. The morphology of test samples were analyzed with a TEM (Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA), which was operated at 200 kV and field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) (FEI Apreo, Czech Co., Ltd., USA). N2 adsorption-desorption isothermal of test samples were obtained by using surface area and porosity analyzer (ASIQM0000000–6, Quantachrome Instruments, USA). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was used to determine specific surface area and total pore volume, t-plot method was used to calculate micropore volume of test samples.

A certain amount of photocatalyst was added into the mixed solution containing acetonitrile and model lignin [28,29], which was performed under dark conditions to avoid light, and kept stirring for 0.5 h to achieve an adsorption-desorption equilibrium prior to photocatalytic reaction. The photocatalytic reaction was carried out under the following conditions: a 300 W Xe lamp as visible light source, O2 as reaction medium, and 25 °C of reaction temperature. During the reaction period, the supernatant is taken and analyzed with triple quadrupole mass spectrometer (Scion TQ, Bruker Inc., USA). The main component in the photocatalytic reaction was quantified according to the following equation:
(1)
C =
C 0
A
A 0
where C is the concentration of each component (μg/L), C
0 is the concentration of internal standard (μg/L), A is the peak area of each component, and A
0 is the peak area of the internal standard.

The Tauc plot can be used to calculate the bandgap of a semiconductor. The E
g was estimated to be using the following:
(2)
αhv = A
hv −
E g
n
where α, h, ν, A and E
g are the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, the frequency, a constant and the band gap energy, respectively. The value of n is determined by whether the optical transition of the semiconductor is direct (n = 1/2) or indirect (n = 2) [30]. Since the optical transformation of g-C3N4 is indirect [31], the value of n is 2.

The element contents of test samples were shown in Table 1 . A large amount of C and O elements, some N elements, and a small amount of P, S and Ca were found in BSKP. Compared with the elements in the CQDs without doping, the contents of both N and P elements in N,P-CQDs were significantly increased, reaching 17.19 % and 7.38 %, respectively, which confirms that N and P elements have been introduced into CQDs.

The diffraction contrast of N,P-CQDs was relatively strong in HRTEM images (Fig. 2a), indicating that the thickness of N,P-CQDs was relatively thick. No obvious lattice was observed, indicating that N,P-CQDs showed amorphous polymer structures [32]. In addition, N, P and S elements were uniformly distributed on the surface of N,P-CQDs (Fig. 2a). In order to fully understand the changes of surface chemical structure of CQDs after doping elements, infrared absorption spectroscopy was used. It can be seen from Fig. 2b that BSKP had obvious characteristic peaks of cellulose skeleton, the strong and wide absorption peak at 3418 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl (OH) on glucose [33]; the absorption peak at 2899 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of CH in cellulose; the absorption peak at 1630 cm−1 was caused by the vibration of carbonyl groups (C=O) of cellulose [22]. Compared with BSKP, the peak intensities of these functional groups on the surface of both CQDs and N,P-CQDs were increased. The weak absorption peak at 1399 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of aromatic ring [34]. Some characteristic peaks of N,P-CQDs were observed, that is, the absorption peak at 3142 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of amino group (NH); the weak absorption band at 1289 cm−1 was related to the existence of C–N; in addition, the chemical bonds such as P–O–C (1099 cm−1) and P–OH (914 cm−1) also appeared in N,P-CQDs [35]. Thus, it can be seen that, N,P-CQDs had abundant functional groups on the surface, which further indicated that element doping can increase the surface defects of CQDs. As can be seen from Fig. 2c that, the optimal excitation wavelength (Ex)/emission wavelength (Em) were found at 325/425 nm for CQDs and 355/450 nm for N,P-CQDs, respectively. After doping N and P, the optimal excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of CQDs were red shifted; meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs was significantly improved in comparison with that of CQDs, confirming that doping N and P greatly improved the photoluminescence performance of resultant CQDs.

UV visible absorption spectra were obtained to explore the influence of N and P doping on light absorption of CQDs. Fig. 2d showed that two samples had obvious characteristic peaks at 270 nm, which was attributed to the n-π* transition of CO bond in CQDs [36]. It was also observed that the absorption range of N,P-CQDs was extended to the visible light region via doping elements, and the absorption intensity was significantly improved, which further indicated that doping both N and P elements was conductive to improving the optical properties of CQDs. Under irradiation of ultraviolet lamp (365 nm), both CQDs and N,P-CQDs displayed blue-green fluorescence, and the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQD was much stronger than CQDs (Fig. 2d). In order to evaluate the stability of CQDs and N,P-CQDs, the influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of two samples was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 2e, f, the change of fluorescence intensity of CQDs and N,P-CQDs was basically consistent within pH 2.0–12.0, that is, the fluorescence intensity increased first and then decreased with the gradual increase of pH value. As we know, the protonation-deprotonation performance varies with pH. The reduction in fluorescence intensity may be resulted from the changes in the ionization degree of the functional groups of CQD/N,P-CQDs with increasing pH, which can affect the degree of deprotonation and thus the fluorescence intensity [37]. However, the fluorescence intensity of N,P-CQDs was much higher than that of CQDs in the range of pH 2.0–12.0, because the doping of N and P atoms can bring about a greater negative charge density, thereby improving photoluminescence [27]. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs was the strongest at pH = 4.0, while N,P-CQDs exhibited the strongest fluorescence at pH 6.0, which confined that doping N and P elements can obviously change the optical properties of CQDs.

The morphologies of the as-prepared composite photocatalysts were characterized using FESEM and TEM. It can be seen that the CN presented a layered structure with interlayer stacking, and a relatively smooth surface without incorporation of CQDs (Fig. 3a-c). In contrast, a large number of N,P-CQDs were uniformly observed on C-SCN surface (Fig. 3d-f). The elemental mapping images also confirmed the evenly distribution of C, N and P elements on C-SCN surface (Fig. 3g), indicating the successful introduction of N,P-CQDs into the composite photocatalyst. These results showed that, g-C3N4 can act as a substrate to attach N,P-CQDs on the surface, and it would also be conductive to inhibiting the aggregation of N,P-CQDs nanoparticles. All of these results confirmed that the C-SCN heterojunctions successfully formed between N,P-CQDs and SCN, which is believed to promote the charges transfer in the interface.

The crystal structure was characterized by using XRD in Fig. 4a. The diffraction peak at 23.7° of N,P-CQDs was originated from the (002) graphite facets [38]. As for SCN and C-SCN, two distinct diffraction peaks at 12.7° and 27.4° were found, which can be assigned to the (001) and (002) crystal planes of g-C3N4, respectively [39], suggesting that both SCN and C-SCN exhibited identical XRD trends. This indicated that the hybridization of g-C3N4 with N,P-CQD did not alter its fundamental crystalline layout, which was consistent with the microscopy images in Fig. 3.

XPS analysis was performed to investigate the chemical state of the elements in the samples. The XPS survey spectra revealed the presence of C, N, O, and P elements in the samples (Fig. 4b). Among them, N,P-CQDs could be clearly observed with signal peaks of N 1 s and P 2p, indicating the presence of N and P. As shown in Fig. 4c, the C 1 s spectra of N,P-CQDs showed three peaks at 284.7, 286.0, and 286.8 eV, which were assigned to C − C/C=C, C − O/C − N, and O − C=O, respectively. The C 1 s spectra suggested the existence of C − C/C=C, C − O/C − N, and N − C=N by observing the signal peaks at 284.6, 286.1, and 288.0 eV in SCN, and at 284.5, 286.0, and 288.0 eV in C-SCN, respectively. It can be seen that, as for C-SCN, the chemical shift occurred towards lower binding energies in comparison with SCN, which indicates that SCN can transfer electrons to N,P-CQDs and would promote the formation of an internal electric field between CN and N,P-CQDs, forming a heterojunction between them [8]. As shown in Fig. 4d, the N1s spectra of N,P-CQDs showed two obvious peaks at 399.6 and 401.4 eV, which were associated with pyridinic N and graphitic N, respectively [40]; meantime, the N 1 s spectra of SCN and C-SCN displayed three prominent peaks at 398.5, 399.9, and 401.2 eV for SCN, and 398.5, 399.8, and 401.1 for C-SCN, which can be assigned to N in sp2 hybridized C − N=C, N − C3, and N − H, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4e, the O 1 s spectra of N,P-CQDs and C-SCN exhibited two strong signal peaks at 530.8 and 532.3 eV, corresponding to CO and C − O, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4f, the P 2p spectrum of N,P-CQDs showed two typical peaks at 133.1 and 133.9 eV, representing P − C/P − N and P − O bond, respectively [41]; meanwhile, nearly the same peaks were found at 132.9 and 133.8 eV in C-SCN, confirming the formation of heterojunction between N,P-CQDs and CN.

The FTIR spectra were obtained to analyze the surface chemical features of CN, SCN and C-SCN. As shown in Fig. 5a, the FTIR spectrum of CN was very similar with that reported in literature [42]. Specifically, the peaks at 1240–1650 cm−1 were the stretching vibration of CN heterocycles; the peak at 3000–3250 cm−1 can be considered as the stretching vibration of N–H; the peak at 801 cm−1 was attributed to the vibration of the triazine ring skeleton. The FTIR spectra of all samples including CN, SCN and C-SCN were basically similar with each other, which suggested that sonication treatment and incorporating N,P-CQDs had no obvious changes on the chemical structure of CN, and the structure of g-C3N4 was not changed by the microscopic heterojunctions formed by N,P-CQDs implantation, which was consistent with the literatures [39,43]. To investigate the photoluminescence (PL) of the as-prepared composite photocatalyst, the PL spectra were obtained and analyzed (Fig. 5b). The results showed that, the as-prepared photocatalysts had wide emission peaks at about 470 nm under the excitation of 345 nm, which corresponded to the combination of photoelectron electrons and holes. When different proportions of N,P-CQDs were compounded into SCN, the fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced, which suggested that N,P-CQDs would promote charge transfer and inhibit the rate of electron hole recombination. Among these samples, the photoelectron emission intensity of C-SCN-2 was much lower than that of CN, indicating that N,P-CQDs had a strong ability to retard charge recombination of CN.

Fig. 5c displayed the UV–visible absorption spectra of as-prepared samples. It can be found that the CN has an absorption wavelength from ultraviolet to visible light region that up to about 450 nm. After coupling with N,P-CQDs, the absorption edge of C-SCN heterojunctions exhibited a red shift when compared with that of CN. This result could be attributed to quantum confinement effect [44]. Meanwhile, when the N,P-CQDs was incorporated to SCN, the visible light absorption of the heterojunctions was greatly improved, which suggested that N,P-CQDs can help to extend the visible spectrum response range of g-C3N4.

As shown in Fig. 5d, the band-gap of the photocatalysts was decreased from 2.85 (CN) to 2.79 eV (C-SCN-2) after incorporating N,P-CQDs. This implied that a sub-band energy level would be formed in the band gaps of the C-SCN heterojunctions (i.e., between SCN and N,P-CQDs) [45], which was consistent with PL spectra results. The N2 adsorption-desorption of the samples were obtained. As can be seen in Fig. 5e, the isotherms were relatively flat at around P/P0 < 0.55, which was attributed to the adsorption that occurred in micropores of samples. Meanwhile, the isotherms also showed an increase in relative pressure (0.55 ≤ P/P0 ≤ 1.00), this is a type-IV isotherm curve with type H3 hysteresis loops, which indicated the presence of mesoporous in all these samples [46]. Interestingly, the hysteresis loop position of each sample was relatively big at a wide large range of relative pressures, which suggested a wide size distribution in all samples, as shown in Fig. 5f. The specific surface area of CN, SCN and C-SCN-2 were determined to be 14.17, 21.62 and 17.62 m2 g−1, respectively. The specific surface area of SCN and C-SCN-2 were much higher than that of CN, which could provide more sites for the photocatalytic reaction, thus improving the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalysts.

As the second most abundant polymers after cellulose in nature [47], lignin demonstrates a great potential to provide inexhaustible supply of aromatic hydrocarbons and other compounds, which can be used as a substitute for fossil resources to prepare high value-added alkane compounds [48,49]. However, the decomposition of complex connections between lignin monomers severely limits its effective depolymerization. Lignin is a complex polymer composed of three basic units including coniferyl alcohol (G-unit), p-coumary alcohol (H-unit) and sinapyl alcohol (S-unit), which are closely linked via several types of CC (i.e., β–1′, β–5′, β–β’) and CO (i.e., β–O–4′, 4–O–5′, α–O–4′) linkages [50]. Among them, β–O–4′ alkyl aromatic ether bond as the main bonding accounts for 50 % ∼ 60 % of all linkages [51]. Therefore, most of research has been focusing on degradin β–O–4′ bond to achieve lignin depolymerization.

In this section, the photocatalytic abilities of the resultant photocatalysts were explored via the comparison between two types of photocatalysts, i.e., SCN and C-SCN. As shown in Table 2, the degradation ratio of SCN for the model lignin was 80.40 %. After incorporating N,P-CQDs, the resultant C-SCN showed an improved photocatalytic ability, reaching up to 95.50 %, increased up to 19 % in comparison with the control (SCN), and the yield of degradation products was also greatly improved. The results suggested that, the resultant photocatalyst effectively cleaved chemical bonds in lignin and promoted the generation of various products. However, it was found that more incorporated CQDs was not better; with incorporating more CQDs, the degradation rate was reduced (i.e., only 75.60 % for C-SCN-3). Thus, it can be concluded that, the photocatalytic capacity of photocatalyst can be reasonably achieved by constructing the heterojunction structures via incorporating a certain amount of N,P-CQDs into g-C3N4. In addition, the photocatalytic products contained some valuable organics such as phenyl formate (2), benzaldehyde (3), and benzoic acid (5), as shown in Fig. 6, which provides a possibility for the production of lignin-derived chemicals. This indicated that C-SCN-2 could not only cause the fracture of β–O–4′ ether bond, but also leads to the degradation of CC bond. In general, the dissociation energy of CC bonds in lignin is generally higher than that of CO bonds, meanwhile the CC bonds locate between bulky aromatic groups, which makes it difficult to be cleaved under normal conditions, resulting in a low activation efficiency [52]. The stability and reusability are essential characteristics of catalysts, so cyclic degradation tests of the as-prepared C-SCN were conducted. As shown in Fig. S1, although the conversion ratio of the model lignin was slightly decreased after each cycle, it remained above 85 % after four cycles. In comparison with other photocatalysts towards lignin degradation, C-CSN-2 had a relatively high degradation ability towards model lignin (Table 3). Besides that, both CQDs and g-C3N4 have some unique advantages such as non-toxic and environmentally friendly features, which is consistent with the concept of sustainable development.

In this section, the photocatalytic abilities of the resultant photocatalysts were explored via the comparison between two types of photocatalysts, i.e., SCN and C-SCN. As shown in Table 2, the degradation ratio of SCN for the model lignin was 80.40 %. After incorporating N,P-CQDs, the resultant C-SCN showed an improved photocatalytic ability, reaching up to 95.50 %, increased up to 19 % in comparison with the control (SCN), and the yield of degradation products was also greatly improved. The results suggested that, the resultant photocatalyst effectively cleaved chemical bonds in lignin and promoted the generation of various products. However, it was found that more incorporated CQDs was not better; with incorporating more CQDs, the degradation rate was reduced (i.e., only 75.60 % for C-SCN-3). Thus, it can be concluded that, the photocatalytic capacity of photocatalyst can be reasonably achieved by constructing the heterojunction structures via incorporating a certain amount of N,P-CQDs into g-C3N4. In addition, the photocatalytic products contained some valuable organics such as phenyl formate (2), benzaldehyde (3), and benzoic acid (5), as shown in Fig. 6, which provides a possibility for the production of lignin-derived chemicals. This indicated that C-SCN-2 could not only cause the fracture of β–O–4′ ether bond, but also leads to the degradation of CC bond. In general, the dissociation energy of CC bonds in lignin is generally higher than that of CO bonds, meanwhile the CC bonds locate between bulky aromatic groups, which makes it difficult to be cleaved under normal conditions, resulting in a low activation efficiency [52]. The stability and reusability are essential characteristics of catalysts, so cyclic degradation tests of the as-prepared C-SCN were conducted. As shown in Fig. S1, although the conversion ratio of the model lignin was slightly decreased after each cycle, it remained above 85 % after four cycles. In comparison with other photocatalysts towards lignin degradation, C-CSN-2 had a relatively high degradation ability towards model lignin (Table 3). Besides that, both CQDs and g-C3N4 have some unique advantages such as non-toxic and environmentally friendly features, which is consistent with the concept of sustainable development.

Based on the abovementioned analysis, a possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism for lignin was proposed, as shown in Fig. 7 . Under simulated solar radiation, C-SCN can generate e− and h+, the electrons were excited from VB to CB. In the constructed heterojunctions (C-SCN), N,P-CQDs can absorb visible light and emit short wavelength light, whose excellent up-conversion ability can not only improve the utilization efficiency of C-SCN for visible light, but also promote the generation and separation of e− and h+. This reduced the band gap of C-SCN and allowed for a rapid transfer of e− on it, and effectively prevent the recombination of photogenerated carriers and improve the activity of C-SCN [39]. Then, e− can undergo a reduction reaction with O2 that adsorbed on C-SCN surface to generate •O2 −, while h+ can undergo an oxidation reaction with OH− to generate highly active •OH. In this process, •O2 −, h+, and •OH can effectively degrade the model lignin into other chemical products.

Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQDs) were successfully fabricated through (NH4)2HPO4 assisted hydrothermal treatment of cellulose pulp fibers. The as-prepared N,P-CQDs were fully characterized and incorporated into g-C3N4 (CN) via facile sonication treatment followed by liquid deposition, thus N,P-CQDs/sonication treated g-C3N4 (C-SCN) composites were fabricated and then explored as photocatalysts. Doping N and P elements significantly improved the optical properties of CQDs, that is, after doping N and P, the optimal excitation and emission wavelength of CQDs were red shifted, the fluorescence intensity and PL performance of N,P-CQDs were significantly improved. When N,P-CQDs was incorporated to SCN, as for the resultant photocatalysts, the visible light absorption was greatly improved, and the absorption edge exhibited a red shift, the band-gap of was reduced from 2.85 (CN) to 2.79 eV (C-SCN-2), the specific surface area was increased. Thus, the resultant C-SCN as photocatalysts showed an improved photocatalytic ability, reaching up to 95.50 %, increased up to 19 % in compared to the control (SCN), meanwhile generating some valuable chemicals such as phenyl formate, benzaldehyde, and benzoic acid. It can be concluded that the cellulose derived CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions greatly enhance the photocatalytic destruction capacity of model lignin, which would broad the application of lignocellulosic biomass derived CQDs in photocatalysis fields such as high value-added transformation of lignin.
